context: And when the people complained, it displeased the Lord. And the Lord heard it, and His anger was kindled, and the fire of the Lord burnt among them and consumed them that were in the uttermost parts of the camp.
point_summary: Israel's complaining displeased and angered God, and the sermon draws this as a warning that a fault-finding, discontented spirit is a universal human tendency, not merely an ancient Israelite failure.

context: The people cried unto Moses, and when Moses prayed unto the Lord, the fire was quenched.
point_summary: God's mercy is seen in Moses's intercessory prayer, which quenched the fire of divine judgment against the complaining Israelites, prompting gratitude that God is gracious and compassionate.

context: and we read how that as they had barely finished seeing God provide miraculously, they would complain about their situation and then God would graciously provide for them and he would prove his power, and yet they would face another difficulty and they would complain, the tendency of complaining. Turn over to the book of Numbers where we are here this morning in chapter number 11, and really much of the remainder of the book of Exodus would communicate for us the 10 commandments, the requirements and the instructions with regard to the tabernacle and all of those things. And of course, the book of Leviticus providing for us much detail with regard to the law. And we come here and really kind of pick up the journey of the children of Israel in Numbers 11. You find that the Bible says, and when the people complained, it was a default setting, it was a tendency for the children of Israel. And it's a tendency in our human nature, and we must be careful,
point_summary: Complaining is identified as a deeply ingrained human tendency, illustrated by Israel's repeated pattern of murmuring even after witnessing God's miraculous provision.

context: Now, and they journeyed from Mount Hor by the way of the Red Sea to compass the land of Edom. It says this, that the soul of the people was much discouraged because of the way.
point_summary: The Israelites' complaint in Numbers 21:4 exposes the self-contradicting irrationality of a complaining spirit: they claimed there was no bread, then in the same breath confessed they despised the miraculous manna God had already provided.

context: Go back to Numbers 11, if you will. I want you to look at the tragedy of complaining.
point_summary: Complaining against God's provision is not merely ingratitude but a direct offense to God, as Numbers 11:1 explicitly states that the people's complaining displeased the Lord.

context: And when the people complained, it displeased the Lord.
point_summary: Complaining is identified as the specific cause of God's displeasure in Numbers 11:1, a tragedy confirmed by Paul's parallel warning against murmuring in 1 Corinthians 10:10.

context: and when the people complained, it displeased the Lord
point_summary: Complaining displeased the Lord and was heard by Him, making it a tragedy precisely because God perceives even what seems private or unspoken among His people.

context: Here in Numbers 11, the Bible tells us that the fire of the Lord burnt among them and consumed them that were in the uttermost parts of the camp.
point_summary: Complaining displeases God and is never hidden from Him, for Numbers 11:1 declares both that it displeased the Lord and that the Lord heard it—making complaint a serious offense inviting divine judgment.

context: Here in Numbers 11, again in Numbers chapter 16, you see that there's a plague as a result.
point_summary: Complaining among God's people is not a minor fault but a sin that historically provoked divine judgment—fire, plague, leprosy, and serpents—demonstrating that God hears and is angered by His people's complaints.

context: Here in Numbers 11, again in Numbers chapter 16, you see that there's a plague as a result.
point_summary: Complaining among Israel's wilderness generation repeatedly drew severe divine judgment, illustrated here by the earth swallowing the complainers in Numbers 16.

context: In Numbers chapter 12, guess what? Miriam and Aaron, they're complaining, they're speaking against Moses, and Miriam is stricken with leprosy.
point_summary: Numbers 12 is cited as one of several Old Testament examples demonstrating that complaining against God's appointed leadership brings severe divine judgment, here specifically Miriam's leprosy.

context: We find that the Bible tells us in Numbers chapter 16 that those that were complaining, that the earth opened up and swallowed them.
point_summary: Numbers 16 is cited as one of several Old Testament examples demonstrating that Israel's complaining provoked severe divine judgment—specifically, the earth opening and swallowing the complainers.

context: In Numbers 21 that we looked at just a moment ago where they were complaining and saying
point_summary: Numbers 21 is cited as one of several Old Testament examples demonstrating that Israel's complaining provoked God's severe judgment—here, fiery serpents sent among the people, resulting in widespread death.

context: the Bible tells us in verse number 6 of Numbers 21 that the Lord sent fiery serpents among the people, and they bit the people and much people of Israel died.
point_summary: God's judgment on Israel's complaining in Numbers 21 — fiery serpents that killed many — is cited as one of several severe Old Testament consequences meant to warn against a complaining spirit.

context: you see that at the end of the chapter, or at the end of the passage in verse number 9, that truth of looking and living.
point_summary: The brazen serpent in Numbers 21:9 — where those bitten looked and lived — is cited as the resolution to divine judgment brought on by Israel's complaining, illustrating the mercy available even within severe consequence.

context: In number 16 in verse number one, God tells us about a rebellion that takes place. And we find that the Bible tells us of this rebellion of Korah.
point_summary: The tribe of Reuben's only notable rise to leadership in Scripture is the catastrophic rebellion of Korah, fulfilling Jacob's prophecy that Reuben would be "unstable as water" and would not excel.

context: in verse number. chapter 16, there's some level of leading with regard to this rebellion that is recorded in number 16.
point_summary: The sole notable leadership role of Reubenites in Scripture is the rebellion of Korah and his associates against Moses in Numbers 16—a catastrophic act of instability that cost thousands their lives and illustrates Jacob's prophecy that Reuben would not excel.

context: you could turn over to Numbers 32 and you could see how they settled on the east side of Jordan.
point_summary: The tribe of Reuben's choice to settle east of the Jordan, cited from Numbers 32, illustrates their pattern of settling for convenience rather than the inheritance God intended.

context: the second census is given, which is recorded for us in Numbers 26, verse number 14 with regard to the Simeonites specifically
point_summary: By the time of the second census in Numbers 26:14, the tribe of Simeon had dramatically declined from 59,300 to 22,200—a decrease of over 37,000—fulfilling Jacob's prophetic word that Simeon would be scattered in Israel.

context: And really, if you look back and you begin to study and you look specifically in Numbers chapter 20, there's a specific incident that is there that provides you with some insight into how that the Edomites really rejoiced at the difficulties and the trials of the Israelites.
point_summary: Numbers 20 is cited as background evidence of the longstanding hostility between Edomites and Israelites, illustrating the ancient enmity prophesied at the birth of Jacob and Esau.

context: we won't take the time this morning to go back to the book of Numbers and to read in the 24th chapter about the star that's mentioned there
point_summary: The star prophecy in Numbers 24 is acknowledged as background to the Magi's recognition of Christ's star, connected to their exposure to Hebrew Scripture through the Babylonian captivity, but left without exposition.

context: our God is not a man that he should lie
point_summary: God's absolute faithfulness to fulfill every promise is grounded in the truth that He is not a man who lies, as confirmed by the resurrection's own testimony: "as he said."

context: God is not a man that he should lie, neither the son of man that he should repent. Hath he said, and shall he not do it? Or hath he spoken, and shall he not make it good?
point_summary: God's faithfulness to His word is absolute—because He is not a man who lies or repents, whatever He has spoken He will without fail make good.

context: if you were to read in Numbers chapter 14, throughout that portion of scripture, when God had Moses send those spies
point_summary: Israel's unbelief at Kadesh-barnea, illustrated by the ten spies' evil report in Numbers 14, is presented as the human limitation that delayed—but could not ultimately nullify—God's promise to bring his people into the land.

context: God tells us about 12 spies that went out to spy the land.
point_summary: The account of the twelve spies in Numbers 13–14 illustrates that people respond differently to adversity: ten were crushed by what they saw and lacked faith, while two trusted God's promise.

context: we're not going to turn there, but in chapter 13 and 14, the book of Numbers
point_summary: The account of the twelve spies in Numbers 13–14 illustrates that people respond differently to adversity: ten were overcome by discouragement while two, Joshua and Caleb, trusted God's promise despite the same circumstances.

context: the people complained and the Lord heard it, and He was displeased
point_summary: Complaining—even about God's miraculous provision, as Israel did with the manna—displeases the Lord, making murmuring a sin no believer can claim exemption from.

context: And do you remember at the end of that portion of scripture in Numbers chapter 14, there was a contingent of them that decided presumptuously that they were going to go up and try to take and enter in.
point_summary: The presumptuous attempt by Israelites to enter Canaan after God withdrew His command—against Moses' warning—illustrates that acting without divine authorization is not faith but self-will, resulting in failure.

context: If you turn to the book of Numbers, Numbers chapter one, notice what the Bible tells us in Numbers one. Numbers chapter one, I want you to see a couple of things. We'll look in chapter one and two, but in Numbers chapter one, notice in verse number 33. Numbers one and verse 33, it says, those that were numbered of them,
point_summary: The census count of Ephraim's tribe at 40,500 confirms that Jacob's deliberate crossing of hands to bless the younger over the older was genuine prophecy, not error.

context: even of the tribe of Ephraim, were 40,500.
point_summary: The census count of Ephraim at 40,500—surpassing Manasseh's 32,200—confirms that Jacob's deliberate crossing of his hands to bless the younger was prophetically fulfilled in Israel's history.

context: In verse 34, the children of Manasseh, by their generations...
point_summary: Jacob's prophecy that Ephraim would surpass Manasseh is confirmed by the census numbers in Numbers 1, where Ephraim's tribe numbered 40,500 against Manasseh's 32,200.

context: even of the tribe of Manasseh, were 32,200.
point_summary: Ephraim's census count of 40,500—surpassing Manasseh's 32,200 by 8,300—confirms that Jacob's crossed-hands blessing of the younger over the elder was genuine prophecy fulfilled in Israel's history.

context: In chapter number two, in verse number 18, Israel and their camp...
point_summary: Numbers 2:18's census and camp arrangement confirm Jacob's prophetic blessing of Ephraim over Manasseh, with Ephraim's larger tribe (40,500 vs. 32,200) and leading position vindicating Jacob's deliberate crossing of hands.

context: God is not a man that he should lie, neither the son of man that he should repent. Hath he said, and shall he not do it? Or hath he spoken, and shall he not make it good?
point_summary: God's absolute faithfulness to every promise He has ever made is grounded in His nature as one who cannot lie, repent, or fail to accomplish what He has spoken.

context: Numbers 23 and verse number 19 tells us that God is not a man that he should lie.
point_summary: God's faithfulness as the ground of his promises is established by citing Numbers 23:19—that God cannot lie—making his character the very basis for the "exceeding great and precious promises" of 2 Peter 1:4.

context: In Numbers 14, as the children of Israel have doubted with that evil report of the ten spies, You give us insight with regard to Your desire for Your people.
point_summary: The wilderness test of Numbers 14—where Israel's unbelief drew God's lament, "How long will it be ere they believe Me?"—is presented as a mirror for examining whether believers today live by faith and obedience.

context: God is not a man that he should lie
point_summary: God's truthfulness—that He does not lie or deceive—is grounded in Numbers 23:19, invoked to counter the Assyrian letter's charge that Hezekiah's God would deceive him.

context: and they, the children of Israel, they journeyed from Mount Hor by the way of the Red Sea to compass the land of Edom, and the soul of the people was much discouraged because of the way.
point_summary: Israel's discouragement arose not from the journey itself but from their resistance to the direction and path God was providing them, which the sermon identifies as the root cause of the problem that followed.

context: God said unto Moses, and the Lord said unto Moses, Make thee a fiery serpent, and set it upon a pole, and it shall come to pass that everyone that is bitten, when he looketh upon it, shall live.
point_summary: God's provision in judgment — commanding Moses to raise a bronze serpent so that every bitten Israelite who looked upon it would live — reveals His grace and mercy operating even within His discipline.

context: With regard to this very account that we're looking at here in Numbers chapter 21, we find that God, the Lord Jesus Christ, says to Nicodemus in verse number 14 of John 3, and as Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, even so must the Son of Man be lifted up. We find that as we would consider the picture here of Numbers chapter 21, we find that it's a picture of the substitutionary death of the Lord Jesus Christ on the cross of Calvary.
point_summary: The bronze serpent lifted on a pole in Numbers 21 is presented as a typological picture of Christ's substitutionary death, directly linked to Jesus' own words to Nicodemus in John 3:14.

context: And we find that as we look at Numbers 21, and we understand there was a condition that was affecting the people, there were fiery serpents and people were dying.
point_summary: The bronze serpent lifted up in Numbers 21 is presented as a typological picture of Christ's substitutionary death on the cross, as Jesus himself draws the parallel in John 3:14.

context: the Bible says, and the people speak against God, and then notice what it says, and against Moses.
point_summary: Israel's complaint in Numbers 21:5 was directed not only against God but against Moses personally, the faithful leader God had placed over them, illustrating the burden borne by those in God-appointed positions of authority.

context: And we find that as Moses is interceding, as he's praying, that the Lord then says unto Moses in verse number eight, notice what he says, make thee a fiery serpent and set it upon a pole. And it shall come to pass that everyone that is bitten when he looketh upon it shall live.
point_summary: God's answer to Moses' intercession in Numbers 21:8 was specific but not what the people requested—rather than removing the serpents, God provided a means of healing through looking upon the bronze serpent.

context: I want you to make a fiery serpent. I want you to set it upon a pole and it shall come to pass that everyone that is bitten when he looketh upon it shall live.
point_summary: God answered Moses's prayer not by removing the serpents as the people requested, but by giving specific, unexpected direction—a fiery serpent on a pole—demonstrating that He answers prayer on His own terms.

context: The Bible tells us just a few chapters back in Numbers chapter 12 and verse number 7,
point_summary: Moses is commended by God himself as faithful "in all mine house," a divine testimony the sermon holds up as the foundation for understanding Moses's immediate obedience and intercession throughout Numbers 21.

context: My servant Moses is not so, who is faithful in all mine house.
point_summary: God's designation of Moses as "faithful in all mine house" (Num. 12:7) is brought forward to identify faithfulness as the character quality underlying Moses's prompt obedience and his communication of God's message to the people in Numbers 21.

context: God said in verse number 8? He said, Moses, make thee a fiery serpent and set it upon a pole, and it shall come to pass that everyone that is bitten, when he looketh upon it, shall live.
point_summary: Moses' faithfulness is demonstrated in his implied communication of God's command—that the bitten must look upon the bronze serpent to live—without which the people could not have known to look and been saved.

context: Moses made a serpent of brass and put it upon a pole, and notice what it says, and it came to pass that if a serpent had bitten any man, when he beheld the serpent of brass, he lived.
point_summary: Moses' obedience in making and lifting the brass serpent is presented as evidence of his faithfulness, since the people could only have known to look upon it because he communicated God's instruction to them.

context: I begin to think about this response of Moses here in the passage of Scripture in Numbers 21, and I begin to see about this man that he interceded for the people.
point_summary: Moses' intercession and obedience in Numbers 21 are presented as the defining marks of faithfulness, illustrated through three Proverbs passages affirming that a faithful man is rare, refreshing, and abundantly blessed.

context: And that's an example that we see in Numbers 21 of Moses.
point_summary: Moses' intercession and obedience in Numbers 21 are presented as the pattern of faithfulness required of every believer—not unique to Moses, but universally expected in prayer, obedience, and stewardship.

context: So this morning as we look at Numbers 21, again, a familiar portion of Scripture, but as we focus in on the behavior of Moses, I hope this morning that we've been encouraged
point_summary: Numbers 21 is presented as a universal pattern: Moses' intercession, obedience, and faithfulness are not unique to his calling but are three responses available to every believer regardless of their particular role.

context: I love in Numbers chapter 11 verse 1, just listen as I read, it's about Moses. It says, And when the people
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: a truth in the book of Numbers that says be sure your sin will find you out.
point_summary: Hidden sin is never truly concealed from God, as Numbers 32:23's warning — "be sure your sin will find you out" — is invoked alongside the examples of Rachel and Achan to underscore that God's omniscience exposes what men attempt to hide.

context: I'm mindful of what the Word of God says in Numbers 14.
point_summary: Choosing to believe the majority over God's word—illustrated by Israel's ten spies and their evil report—is framed as a recurring human failure that God never desired.

context: In Numbers chapter number 13, the Bible gives us the record of the spies going to spy out the land.
point_summary: Faced with conflicting reports from the twelve spies, Israel chose to believe the majority's evil report rather than God, establishing the same pattern of misplaced trust the centurion repeats in Acts 27.

context: And in Numbers 13, they bring up this evil report.
point_summary: Despite witnessing repeated miraculous provision, Israel responded to the spies' evil report in Numbers 13 by rejecting God's demonstrated faithfulness and threatening to stone those who urged trust in Him.

context: And in verse 7 it says, and they spake in Numbers 14, and they spake unto all the company of the children of Israel, saying, the land which we pass through to search it is an exceeding good land.
point_summary: Caleb and Joshua's appeal in Numbers 14:7–9 frames the promised land as attainable not by Israel's strength but by the Lord's delight and presence, making the people's rebellion a failure of belief rather than circumstance.

context: He said in Numbers 14, when He speaks to Moses, How long will it be ere my people believe me?
point_summary: God's patience with unbelief is pressed into urgent personal application through Numbers 14:11's question — "How long will it be ere my people believe me?" — as a warning against 2021 Christians failing to trust a God who has repeatedly proven Himself.

context: you then come to Numbers chapters 3 and 4, and you see that it's the tribe of Levi that is established as the tribe in which the priest would come from.
point_summary: Numbers 3–4 establishes that the Levitical priesthood was divinely restricted by tribe and lineage, so that no one could assume the priestly office apart from God's specific appointment.

context: you then come to Numbers chapters 3 and 4, and you see that it's the tribe of Levi that is established as the tribe in which the priest would come from.
point_summary: Numbers 3–4 establishes that priestly lineage was divinely fixed to the tribe of Levi, so that even Levites outside the Aaronic line were excluded from the priesthood—a pattern underscoring that no man takes this honor to himself.

context: And you see in Numbers chapter 16 there with Korah, and they were of the tribe of Levi, but they had been given specific roles to fulfill.
point_summary: Numbers 16 is cited as a negative example demonstrating that the priestly office required divine appointment, not self-selection — Korah's rebellion and its judgment by God illustrating the consequence of assuming a role one was not ordained to fill.

context: as God is speaking to Moses in the book of Numbers in chapter 14 and verse number 11, if you want the reference, but God is speaking to Moses and that passage
point_summary: God's repeated, sign-accompanied faithfulness to Israel still failed to produce belief, illustrating that a lack of faith is not merely a practical failure but an affront to God that He directly questions.

context: it was through the counsel of Balaam that he encouraged the people of God to corrupt themselves
point_summary: Balaam's counsel in Numbers 31:16, by which Israel was led into self-corruption rather than direct cursing, illustrates how the enemy escalates deception when outright opposition fails to stop God's work.

context: Begin to read about that in Numbers chapter 22 through 25.
point_summary: Numbers 22–25 is introduced as the background narrative where Balaam, hired to curse Israel, instead blesses them—establishing the context for understanding the "doctrine of Balaam" condemned in Revelation 2:14.

context: but turn back if you will to Numbers chapter 25.
point_summary: The "doctrine of Balaam" condemned in Revelation 2:14 is traced to Numbers 25, where the strategy Balaam taught Balak—leading Israel into idolatry and fornication—is shown in its actual historical outworking.

context: And Israel abode in Shittim, and the people began to commit whoredom with the daughters of Moab.
point_summary: Numbers 25:1 is read as the historical record of Israel's sexual immorality and idolatry with Moab—the outcome the sermon identifies as what the "doctrine of Balaam" actually produced in practice.

context: And they called the people unto the sacrifice of their gods, and the people did eat and bowed down to their gods.
point_summary: Numbers 25:2 is read as the historical record of Israel's sin — sexual immorality and idolatrous worship with Moabite women — which the sermon identifies as the outworking of Balaam's counsel before that counsel is explicitly named in Numbers 31.

context: And Israel joined himself unto Baal Peor, and the anger of the Lord was kindled against Israel.
point_summary: Israel's joining to Baal-Peor through sexual immorality and idolatry (Num. 25:1–3) is presented as the historical content of the "doctrine of Balaam" condemned in the letter to Pergamos.

context: Balak's anger was kindled against Balaam
point_summary: Balak's fury at Balaam in Numbers 24:10 sets the narrative tension that makes Israel's sudden fall into idolatry and whoredom in chapter 25 so jarring, pointing the reader toward Balaam's hidden counsel as the explanation.

context: 'And Moses said unto them, 'Have you saved all the women alive?'
point_summary: Numbers 31:15–16 reveals that Balaam, unable to curse Israel directly, instead counseled Balak to use Moabite women to seduce Israel into idolatry and sexual sin, explaining the otherwise puzzling events of Numbers 25.

context: In verse 16 of numbers 31, it's referring to that event that we just read in chapter 25, verses one through three.
point_summary: Numbers 31:16 is cited to establish that Balaam's strategy was not direct cursing but corrupting counsel—leading Israel into compromise so that the resulting judgment functioned as the curse he could not pronounce directly.

context: it's referring to that event that we just read in chapter 25, verses one through three.
point_summary: Numbers 25:1 anchors the argument that Israel's fall into whoredom and idolatry was not the result of a direct curse but of Balaam's counsel to corrupt the people through compromise, which then brought the curse upon them.

context: the anger of the Lord was kindled against Israel
point_summary: Because God's blessing on his people cannot be overturned by a curse, the enemy's strategy—illustrated by Balaam's counsel in Numbers 25—is to corrupt them into sin, which forfeits that blessing and invites divine judgment instead.

context: Reminds me of what the Lord said to Moses back in numbers chapter 14
point_summary: Israel's faithlessness at Kadesh-barnea, contrasted with Caleb and Joshua's confident trust in God's promise, illustrates the spectrum between great faith and no faith that the sermon traces across Scripture.

context: but in numbers 13 God instructs Moses to send the spies
point_summary: Faith's presence or absence is illustrated by contrasting the believing reports of Caleb and Joshua with the fearful majority in Numbers 13, as a backdrop to God's rebuke in Numbers 14:11 about Israel's unbelief.

context: God is speaking to Moses and he says this the Lord said unto Moses How long will this people provoke me and how long will it be air? They believe me For all the signs which I have showed Among them
point_summary: God's repeated signs among Israel make their unbelief a personal affront to him, illustrating that persistent doubt provokes the Lord who has already proven himself trustworthy.

context: Think about the results of unbelief for the children of Israel in Numbers 14.
point_summary: Unbelief carries severe consequences, as Numbers 14 shows Israel forfeiting the promised land through faithlessness, while Joshua and Caleb alone were spared—yet still delayed forty years by the nation's sin.

context: And then later in the passage in v. 23-24 of Numbers 14, the Bible says, "...surely they shall not see the land which I swear unto their fathers, neither shall any of them that provoked Me see it."
point_summary: Unbelief carries severe consequences: those who provoked God at Kadesh were barred from the Promised Land entirely, dying in the wilderness rather than inheriting what God had sworn to their fathers.

context: and remember he had mentioned in chapter number 3 about that event That's recorded in numbers 13 and 14 when those 12 spies went out and the 10 came back with that evil report
point_summary: Israel's failure at Kadesh-Barnea (Numbers 13–14), where ten spies returned an evil report rooted in unbelief, illustrates the Hebrews warning that hearing God's word without faith renders it unprofitable.

context: and remember he had mentioned in chapter number 3 about that event That's recorded in numbers 13 and 14 when those 12 spies went out and the 10 came back with that evil report
point_summary: Israel's failure at Kadesh-Barnea (Numbers 13–14), cited in Hebrews 3–4, illustrates the danger of hearing God's word without mixing it with faith, serving as the backdrop for the second major warning passage.

context: We are mindful, maybe one of the most familiar accounts is found for us in Numbers 20, verses 14 through 22, and I'm just going to briefly mention
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the people spake against the Lord and He sent fiery serpents
point_summary: Murmuring against God's provision — illustrated by Israel's complaint about manna in Numbers 21:5 — is presented as sin that displeased God and provoked divine judgment, warning believers that God hears complaints as surely as He hears praise.

context: when the people complained, it displeased the Lord and the Lord heard it and His anger was kindled
point_summary: Murmuring and complaining displeased God and provoked His judgment against Israel, serving as a warning that God hears complaints just as He hears praise.

context: A very familiar one is in Numbers chapter 14, after they're ready to stone Joshua and Caleb and all that takes place and God's judgment is pronounced, and Moses goes before the Lord.
point_summary: Moses interceding for Israel after the rebellion against Joshua and Caleb is cited as a biblical model of praying on behalf of others rather than oneself.

context: ...the truth of Numbers chapter 25 and all that was going on...
point_summary: Numbers 25:1 is brought forward as the Old Testament backdrop to the "doctrine of Balaam," showing that the sins of fornication and idolatry condemned in Thyatira had a precise historical precedent in Israel's corruption at Shittim.

context: Notice in chapter 25, in verse number 1, and Israel bowed and chit them, and the people began to commit whoredom with the daughters of Moab.
point_summary: Numbers 25:1 illustrates the doctrine of Balaam: though Israel could not be cursed directly, Balaam's counsel led them to corrupt themselves through fornication and idolatry, bringing God's judgment upon them.

context: woman in the sight of Moses, and in the sight of all the congregation of the children of Israel, who were weeping before the door of the tabernacle of the congregation.
point_summary: Phinehas's violent intervention against brazen covenant-breaking in Numbers 25:6 illustrates that God's judgment on unrepentant sin, though sometimes delayed, is both certain and severe.

context: And when Phinehas, the son of Eleazar, the son of Aaron, the priest, saw it, he rose up from among the congregation, and took a javelin in his hand,
point_summary: Phinehas's zealous act of judgment against Israel's sexual and idolatrous sin at Baal-peor illustrates that God's judgment on unrepentant corruption is both certain and severe.

context: and he went after the man of Israel into the tent, and thrust both of them through, the man of Israel and the woman through her belly. So the plague was stayed from the children of Israel.
point_summary: Phinehas's act of zealous judgment that stopped the plague—resulting in twenty-four thousand dead—illustrates that God's judgment on unrepentant sin is both certain and severe.

context: But then notice verse 9, and those that died in the plague were twenty and four thousand.
point_summary: The death toll of twenty-four thousand in the plague at Baal-peor illustrates that God's judgment on sexual immorality and idolatry is both certain and severe, a pattern the sermon applies directly to the church at Thyatira.

context: The Lord is long-suffering, and of great mercy, forgiving iniquity and transgression, and by no means clearing the guilty, visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children unto the third and fourth generation.
point_summary: Moses quotes God's own self-description of long-suffering and great mercy alongside justice as the theological ground on which the intercessory plea of verse 19 is built.

context: Pardon, I beseech thee, the iniquity of this people, according unto the greatness of thy mercy, and as thou hast forgiven this people, from Egypt even until now.
point_summary: Moses's intercessory plea in Numbers 14:19 grounds the appeal for pardon not in Israel's merit but explicitly in the greatness of God's mercy, a phrase the sermon takes as its governing title and year-long theological theme.
