context: As we begin to think about the text of scripture that we're in here this morning in Numbers 14, and we're looking at just the center section, if you will, of this particular chapter...
point_summary: Moses's intercession in Numbers 14 appeals specifically to the greatness of God's mercy as the ground for pardoning Israel's unbelief after the ten spies brought an evil report from Canaan.

context: It was in Numbers chapter 13 that God had given the instruction to Moses to send those 12 spies into the land...
point_summary: In Numbers 13, the twelve spies were commissioned not to assess whether Israel could conquer Canaan but simply to bring back a report, yet ten returned with an evil report of walled cities and formidable defenses.

context: Joshua and Caleb stand up after the evil report
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the Bible tells us of number 14, that the people are gonna stone Joshua and Caleb
point_summary: Israel's rejection of Caleb and Joshua's faithful report—culminating in the threat to stone them—provokes God's direct rebuke that his people refused to believe him despite all the signs he had shown them.

context: the Bible tells us that the Lord then speaks to Moses in verse 11, and notice what God says, how long will this people provoke me?
point_summary: Despite all the signs God had shown Israel — from Egypt through the Red Sea — their refusal to trust his promise provoked him, exposing unbelief as the root of their rebellion.

context: Isn't it amazing, as you read the account in Numbers chapter 14, our text for this morning, that when we deal with this truth, as we look through this passage of scripture, we notice first what Moses recalled...
point_summary: In Numbers 14, Moses' intercession is grounded in deliberate recollection of God's previously revealed character—specifically the self-proclamation of Exodus 34—as the basis for appealing to divine mercy.

context: ...we find that here in this passage of scripture in Numbers 14...
point_summary: In Numbers 14, Moses' intercession succeeds because he recalls what God had previously revealed—demonstrating that effective prayer depends on being a student of Scripture, since one cannot recall what one does not know.

context: As we look at the passage of Scripture here in Numbers 14, we note first what Moses recalled and what he requested, but now I want you to notice as we finish the passage of Scripture here in verse number 20, I want you to notice how God responded.
point_summary: God's response to Moses' intercession in Numbers 14:20 is presented as the direct, gracious answer to a bold, undeserved request for pardon grounded solely in divine mercy, not human merit.

context: I see in the passage of Scripture this morning in Numbers 14 how Moses recalls this and he stands as an intercessor between the people and God.
point_summary: In Numbers 14, Moses stands as an intercessor between a rebellious people and God, illustrating that divine mercy withholds deserved judgment — a pattern the sermon connects to God's saving grace in the New Testament.

context: ...you think about the wise men with the star, and they see about that truth really from Numbers chapter 24.
point_summary: Numbers 24 is cited briefly as the Old Testament source behind the star that the wise men followed, without further exposition in this segment.

context: God asked Moses in Numbers chapter 14, how long will it be, are my people believe me?
point_summary: Doubt is identified as a primary hindrance to forward obedience, grounded in God's own question to Moses in Numbers 14 — "How long will it be, are my people believe me?" — as evidence that God desires faith, not doubt, from His people.

context: how long will it be ere my people believe them?
point_summary: Israel's failure at Kadesh-barnea illustrates how unbelief and the influence of a fearful majority report can prevent God's people from going forward in obedience to His direct command.

context: In chapters 13 and 14 of the book of Numbers, God has Moses take 12 spies and send them into the land to see what's there.
point_summary: When God commands forward movement, the influence of fearful voices — like the ten spies' evil report — must yield to the obedient faith of those who trust the command regardless of visible obstacles.

context: In chapters 13 and 14 of the book of Numbers, God has Moses take 12 spies and send them into the land to see what's there.
point_summary: Israel's failure to go forward into Canaan illustrates how the influence of fearful, doubting voices can override God's direct command to advance in obedience and faith.

context: neither let us commit fornication as some of them committed and fell in one day, three and 20,000. Wow. That would be the record back in Numbers chapter 25.
point_summary: Israel's catastrophic fall into fornication at Shittim (Numbers 25), costing 23,000 lives in a single day, is cited as a historical warning against sexual sin that believers must heed.

context: Neither let us tempt Christ, as some of them also tempted and were destroyed of serpents.
point_summary: Israel's murmuring against God's provision in Numbers 21—resulting in destruction by fiery serpents—is identified as tempting Christ, meaning they put God's character and power to proof by challenging His gracious provision.

context: They said this in Numbers 21, in that passage of Scripture that's being referenced
point_summary: Israel's murmuring against Moses and God in Numbers 21 — complaining about the manna and questioning the exodus — is identified as tempting Christ by putting God's character and power to proof.

context: I believe that it's number 16 that is being referenced here in verse number 10
point_summary: Numbers 16 is identified as the likely background for the warning against murmuring in 1 Corinthians 10:10, illustrating that Israel's chronic complaining challenged the very goodness and character of God.

context: And God, in fact, says in Numbers 14, when he's speaking to Moses, how long will it be, e'er my people believe me?
point_summary: The irony that Jericho's pagan inhabitants trembled at God's mighty acts while Israel's own people refused to believe—prompting God's rebuke in Numbers 14—sharpens the call for believers to respond to God's works with faith rather than fear.

reference_text: God is not a man that He should lie
context: We must understand that God is not a man that He should lie.
point_summary: God's promises are only as trustworthy as the one who makes them, and because God is not a man that He should lie, His promises can be fully trusted.

context: We find in Numbers chapter 35 in verses nine through 34 that God gives the instruction to Moses...
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the Lord spake unto Moses, saying, Speak unto the children of Israel, and say unto them, When ye be come over Jordan into the land of Canaan, then ye shall appoint you cities to be cities of refuge for you.
point_summary: Numbers 35:9–14 is cited to show that God's foundational principle against murder in Genesis 9 was later refined through the cities of refuge, which distinguished accidental killing from intentional murder pending judgment before the congregation.

context: you shall give three cities on this side Jordan, and three cities shall ye give in the land of Canaan, which shall be cities of refuge
point_summary: Numbers 35:14 establishes the geographic distribution of the six cities of refuge—three on each side of the Jordan—available to Israelites, strangers, and sojourners who kill someone unawares.

context: Noah after they would depart the ark, in verse 17 of numbers 35, it says, and if he smite him with the throwing a stone, wherewith he may die, and he die, he is a murderer. The murderer shall surely be put to death.
point_summary: Intentional use of a deadly weapon — even a thrown stone — constitutes murder under Mosaic law, grounding the death penalty in the Genesis 9 mandate given to Noah.

context: Verse 18, or if he smite him with an hand weapon or wood, wherewith he may die, and he die, he is a murderer. The murderer shall surely be put to death.
point_summary: Striking with a hand weapon of wood that results in death constitutes murder under Mosaic law, grounded in the capital-punishment mandate God established with Noah in Genesis 9.

context: God asks Moses in the book of Numbers after the spies go out and they come back with that evil report in Numbers 14, God says to Moses, how long will it be ere My people believe Me?
point_summary: God's question to Moses in Numbers 14:11 — "How long will it be ere My people believe Me?" — is cited to show that despite overwhelming evidence of His faithfulness, Israel still doubted, underscoring the call to trust God at all times.

context: You could jot down in your notes Numbers chapter 15 verses 30 and 31 and reference that
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: You could jot down in your notes Numbers chapter 15 verses 30 and 31 and reference that
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: he's not a man that he should lie
point_summary: God's immutability and inability to lie—anchored in Numbers 23:19's declaration that God is not a man who should lie—are presented as the twin foundations confirming the surety of his covenant promises.

context: But here in Numbers chapter number 20, we find this dispute that is listed here with the Edomites or the people of Edom, the descendants of Esau.
point_summary: Numbers 20 is cited as the first in a series of historical disputes between Israel and Edom, tracing the ancient sibling rivalry—rooted in the womb struggle of Jacob and Esau—into the wilderness period, where Israel's reasonable request for passage was refused.

context: They've already disobeyed God, according to the evil report that was brought in Numbers chapter 14 and they said they weren't able.
point_summary: Numbers 14 is referenced only as background context establishing Israel's prior disobedience before the Numbers 20 dispute with Edom, without further exposition in this segment.

context: we find in verse number 14, here in Numbers 20, the Bible says that Moses sent messengers from Cadiz unto the king of Edom.
point_summary: Numbers 20:14 introduces the first of several historical disputes between Israel and Edom, illustrating how the ancient struggle between Jacob and Esau continued through their descendants as nations.

context: and the Egyptians vexed us and our fathers. And when we cried unto the Lord, he heard our voice and sent an angel and hath brought us forth out of Egypt. And behold, we are in Cadiz, a city in the outermost of thy border.
point_summary: Numbers 20:14–18 is read to show that Israel's humble, non-intrusive request for passage through Edom was met with a hostile refusal, illustrating the deep-seated animosity the descendants of Esau bore toward the descendants of Jacob.

context: Let us pass, I pray thee, through thy country. We will not pass through the fields or through the vineyards neither will we drink of the water of the wells. We will only go by the king's highway. We will not turn to the right hand nor to the left until we have passed thy borders.
point_summary: Israel's modest, non-intrusive request to pass through Edom's territory along the king's highway was met with a hostile refusal, revealing the deep ancestral animosity between Esau's descendants and Jacob's.

context: Specifically goes back to Numbers 20. To the event that we read of in Numbers 20 where they said we're gonna pass through.
point_summary: Obadiah's indictment against Edom traces directly back to the Numbers 20 incident, where Edom refused Israel passage, establishing the foundational offense underlying all subsequent prophetic judgment against Esau's descendants.

context: They complained about the provision of God and they said we loathe this white bread
point_summary: Israel's complaint against the manna in Numbers 11 is cited as a warning that God does not hold innocent those who despise his faithful, daily provision even amid hardship.

context: You read numbers 14 and you can read how the judgment of God was announced.
point_summary: Israel's failure at Kadesh-barnea illustrates that the problem was not the absence of God's word or faithful preaching, but the hearers' refusal to receive what was plainly declared to them.

context: He's not a man that he should lie
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: they said in Numbers 14, we be not able
point_summary: Moses's intercession in Numbers 14 is cited as evidence that God's mercy endures forever, even when His people doubt and refuse to advance, nearly provoking His judgment.

context: it was the end of chapter 13 that they make that statement
point_summary: God's enduring mercy is the very attribute Moses appealed to when interceding for Israel after their unbelief at the border of Canaan, demonstrating that this mercy is not abstract but proven in history's darkest moments of failure.

context: verse 17 of numbers 14 and now I beseech thee
point_summary: Moses's intercessory prayer in Numbers 14:17, pleading that God's power be shown through His long-suffering and great mercy, illustrates the enduring mercy that grounds the psalmist's praise in Psalm 118.

context: let the power of my Lord be great according as thou hast spoken saying the Lord is long-suffering and of great mercy
point_summary: Moses's intercessory plea in Numbers 14:17—begging God to act according to His own declared character as long-suffering and of great mercy—illustrates why God's mercy endures forever and why His people have perpetual cause for praise.

context: we finished our lesson this morning with that truth from Numbers 11 in verse number one, that the people complained and it displeased the Lord and the Lord heard it and his anger was kindled against them
point_summary: God's displeasure when his people complain, as recorded in Numbers 11:1, is set against the biblical call to thankfulness, with the text cited to show that complaining is sin, not merely weakness.

context: I don't think he knew in Numbers 13 and 14
point_summary: Joshua's courage in Numbers 13, when he refused to deny the giants and walled cities yet declared "our God is able," is presented as the same faith now sustained by God's renewed promise of presence at the opening of his leadership.

context: I don't think he knew in Numbers 13 and 14
point_summary: Joshua and Caleb's minority report in Numbers 13–14 is held up as the model of faith that acknowledges real dangers—walled cities, giants, sons of Anak—while maintaining that God's presence makes him able to overcome them.

context: Go to the book of Numbers in chapter 18
point_summary: Old Testament priests served the Lord in the tabernacle and temple, and the Levitical model in Numbers 18 is introduced to illuminate what it means for believers, as a royal priesthood, to serve the Lord.

context: now the Lord said unto Aaron, thou and thy sons and thy father's house with thee shall bear the iniquity of the sanctuary. And thou and thy sons with thee shall bear the iniquity of your priesthood. And thy brethren also of the tribe of Levi, the tribe of thy father, bring thou with thee that they may be joined unto thee and minister unto thee. But thou and thy sons with thee shall minister before the tabernacle of witness. They shall keep thy charge and the charge of all the tabernacle. Only they shall not come nigh the vessels of the sanctuary
point_summary: Priestly service in the tabernacle was restricted by birth into the tribe of Levi, illustrating that only those born into God's family—as New Testament believers are through regeneration—can truly serve Him.

context: And they shall be joined unto thee and keep the charge of the tabernacle, the congregation for all the service of the tabernacle
point_summary: Only those born into the priesthood could lawfully serve at the sanctuary; the Levitical warning that "a stranger shall not come nigh" illustrates that true service requires the right standing before God.

context: And a stranger shall not come nigh unto you
point_summary: Priestly service in the Old Testament was restricted to those set apart by God, illustrating that true service to the Lord requires being born into the right standing before Him.

context: And you shall keep the charge of the sanctuary and the charge of the altar that there be no wrath anymore upon the children of Israel
point_summary: Priestly service in the tabernacle was a gift given exclusively to Aaron's line, and any unauthorized person—however powerful—who drew near was subject to death, illustrating that true service to God requires proper standing before Him.

context: I have taken your brethren, notice the Levites, from among the children of Israel to you, they are given as a gift for the Lord
point_summary: Priestly service belongs only to those divinely appointed to it, as the Levites were given as a gift to God for tabernacle service, and no unauthorized person—however prominent—may approach without consequence.

context: Therefore, thou and thy sons with thee shall keep your priest office for everything of the altar and within the veil and ye shall serve. I have given your priest office unto you as a service of gift and the stranger that cometh nigh shall be put to death
point_summary: Priestly service belongs exclusively to those appointed by God, and the unauthorized—however powerful or well-intentioned—have no right to approach what God has reserved for his ordained ministers.

context: as we look at Numbers 18, we begin to see the Old Testament priest
point_summary: Numbers 18's strict boundaries on priestly office — illustrated by Uzziah's leprosy for unauthorized altar service — point forward to Christ as the true High Priest after the order of Melchizedek, not Aaron.

context: during the life and ministry of the Lord Jesus Christ, it reminds us of the children of Israel in Numbers 14 when they did not believe God
point_summary: Numbers 14's account of Israel's unbelief is invoked as a negative counterpart to Eleazar's faith-filled prayer, illustrating that prayerless unbelief forfeits God's mighty works.

context: Numbers chapter 11
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: in the book of Numbers, you read chapters 13 and 14, there was doubt, there was fear, there was unbelief
point_summary: Israel's unbelief in Numbers 13–14, driven by the ten faithless spies, caused an entire generation to forfeit the promised land and wander forty years in the wilderness.

context: in the book of Numbers, you read chapters 13 and 14, there was doubt, there was fear, there was unbelief
point_summary: Israel's unbelief and the ten spies' negative report in Numbers 13–14 caused the entire congregation to refuse entering Canaan, resulting in forty years of wilderness wandering before Joshua led them into the promised land.

context: the Bible tells us in chapter 21 of the book of Numbers
point_summary: Israel's repeated murmuring in Numbers, illustrated by chapter 21, contrasts sharply with what God is worthy to receive—praise—by showing he was instead receiving his people's complaints.

context: Brother Mark was in verse chapter 14
point_summary: Israel's repeated murmuring in Numbers, illustrated here by chapter 14, contrasts sharply with what God is worthy to receive, showing that his people were giving him complaints rather than praise.

context: In Numbers 21, the Bible tells us that they journeyed in verse number 4
point_summary: Israel's repeated murmuring in Numbers 21:4 illustrates that God's people often return complaints rather than praise to the God who visibly leads them.

context: the soul of the people was much discouraged because of the way
point_summary: Israel's soul grew "much discouraged because of the way," illustrating how God's people can murmur against His own miraculous provision even while being visibly led by Him.

context: the people spake against God and against Moses, wherefore have you brought us up out of Egypt to die in the wilderness? For there is no bread, neither is there any water, and our soul loatheth this light bread
point_summary: Israel's complaint in Numbers 21:5 — claiming there was no bread while simultaneously despising the manna God miraculously provided — illustrates the self-contradicting absurdity of murmuring against God's provision.

context: The Lord sent fiery serpents among the people, and they bit the people, in much people of Israel died
point_summary: Murmuring against God's leadership and provision—despite witnessing his miraculous sustenance—brought divine judgment in the form of fiery serpents, demonstrating that God is worthy of praise, not complaint.

context: We saw the Giants in the land the sons of Anak
point_summary: The report of giants in Canaan (Num. 13:33) is explained not as evidence of supernatural cohabitation after the flood but as natural genetic variation already present within the human DNA code.

context: numbers 13 and 14
point_summary: The account of the twelve spies in Numbers 13–14 is introduced as a case study in how doubt and unbelief enter the believer's life and limit what God is able to do on their behalf.

context: numbers 13 and 14
point_summary: Numbers 13–14, the account of the twelve spies, is introduced as a case study in how doubt and unbelief enter the believer's life and limit what God is able to do on their behalf.

context: we look here in these portions of scripture in numbers 13 and 14
point_summary: The narrative of the twelve spies in Numbers 13–14 serves as the primary case study for identifying how doubt and unbelief enter a believer's life and limit what God is able to do.

context: we look here in these portions of scripture in numbers 13 and 14
point_summary: The narrative of the twelve spies in Numbers 13–14 is used as the primary case study for identifying how personal doubt and unbelief enter and limit what God is able to do in a believer's life.

context: as we look at the portion of scripture here in Numbers 13 and 14, and we'll primarily this evening be in chapter 13
point_summary: Numbers 13–14 is introduced as the biblical case study for diagnosing the root causes of doubt, before the sermon turns to its consequences and conquest.

context: as we look at the portion of scripture here in Numbers 13 and 14
point_summary: Numbers 13–14 is introduced as a case study for identifying the causes of doubt, with the surrounding analogy framing doubt as a weed that must be recognized before it can be removed.

context: the Bible says in verse 26, and they went and came to Moses and to Aaron and to all the congregation of the children of Israel into the wilderness of Paran to Kadesh
point_summary: Numbers 13:26 introduces the spies' return with tangible evidence of the land's abundance, setting the stage for examining how doubt can arise even when God's promises are visibly confirmed.

context: they brought back some grapes and the one cluster of grapes, it was so large that they bear it between two upon a staff
point_summary: The enormous cluster of grapes carried on a pole between two men confirmed the land's abundance, yet that very evidence of God's faithfulness became the backdrop against which the spies' "nevertheless" revealed a faulty perspective driving their doubt.

context: we came unto the land whither thou sentest us. And surely it floweth with milk and honey, and this is the fruit of it
point_summary: Doubt begins not with outright denial but with a pivot—the spies' initial affirmation that the land flows with milk and honey is immediately undercut by the word "nevertheless," marking the moment a true report gives way to a faulty perspective.

context: Nevertheless, things are about to change direction
point_summary: The pivotal word "nevertheless" in verse 28 marks the precise moment the spies' report shifts from confirmed blessing to doubt-producing fear, illustrating how a faulty perspective overrides acknowledged truth.

context: the people be strong that dwell in the land. The cities are walled and very great
point_summary: The pivotal word "nevertheless" in verse 28 marks the moment the spies' perspective shifts from God's promised provision to the overwhelming obstacles before them, illustrating how faulty perspective is the root of doubt.

context: it flows with milk and honey
point_summary: The spies' initial affirmation that the land flows with milk and honey serves as the positive baseline that makes the pivotal word "nevertheless" in verse 28 the turning point where faulty perspective replaces God-centered faith with obstacle-centered doubt.

context: In verse 29, the Amalekites dwell in the land of the South and the Hittites and the Jebusites and the Amorites dwell in the mountains and the Canaanites dwell by the sea and by the coast of Jordan.
point_summary: Doubt takes root when perspective shifts from the Lord to the obstacles — the enumerated enemies of verse 29 becoming evidence of a faulty focus rather than occasions for faith.

context: The conclusion that they reach in verse 31, this logic of theirs is faulty logic because they had a faulty perspective. The Bible tells us in verse 31
point_summary: Faulty perspective produces faulty logic: the spies' conclusion in verse 31 that Israel "be not able" to take the land is traced directly to their wrong focus, not to the actual facts before them.

context: the men that went up with him said, notice what they say, we be not able to go up against this people for they are stronger than we
point_summary: Faulty perspective produces faulty logic: the spies' conclusion in verse 31 — "we be not able" — exceeded their assigned task, which never asked them to determine capability but only to bring a report.

context: and see the land what it is. The people that dwelleth therein, whether they be strong or weak, few or many, and what the land is that they dwell in, whether it be good or bad, and what cities they be that they dwell in, whether in tents or in strongholds, and what the land is, whether it be fat or lean, whether there be wood therein or not, and be of good courage and bring of the fruit of the land
point_summary: The spies' assigned task in Numbers 13:18 was strictly to observe and report, not to render a verdict on Israel's ability to conquer—making their conclusion in verse 31 a product of faulty perspective, not faithful obedience.

context: as you begin reading the 14th chapter, you find that what happens is they're ready to go back to Egypt
point_summary: The ten spies' doubt arose not from a false report but from a wrong focus—their assigned task never required them to assess their own ability, only to observe and return with fruit.

context: the people are strong, the cities are walled, the giants are there, the sons of Anak are there, and we be not able to go up against the people
point_summary: The ten spies exceeded their assigned task by rendering a verdict of inability, when God had only commissioned them to observe and report, revealing that doubt arises from a wrong focus rather than wrong facts.

context: Caleb, he's ready to have this perspective that we are well able to overcome it at the end of verse number 30
point_summary: Caleb's confidence in Numbers 13:30 rested not on disputing the spies' accurate report of walled cities and giants, but on a God-centered focus that refused to let visible obstacles determine the conclusion.

context: Joshua was one of the two good spies that we mentioned in numbers 13 and 14
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Take in Numbers 13 and verse number one
point_summary: God's declaration in Numbers 13:1—"which I give unto the children of Israel"—frames the spies' mission not as a scouting venture into uncertain territory but as reconnaissance into a land already promised, making their later conclusion "we be not able" a failure of memory regarding God's prior commitment.

context: verse number two, send thou men that they may search the land of Canaan which I give unto the children of Israel of every tribe of their fathers shall ye send
point_summary: God's own declaration — "which I give" — frames Canaan as an already-granted promise, making Israel's later conclusion "we be not able" a failure of memory and trust, not an accurate reading of reality.

context: how long will this people provoke me? And how long will it be ere they believe me?
point_summary: Doubt takes root when God's people forget his past works and promises, leaving them unable to believe a God whose perfect faithfulness gives every reason for trust.

context: how long will it be ere... they believe me. But notice what he says, for all the signs which I have showed among them
point_summary: Doubt, as seen in Israel's repeated failure at Numbers 14:11, is rooted in forgetting God's demonstrated works—works God performed precisely to build faith for the next step of obedience.

context: just a few things from Numbers 13
point_summary: Numbers 13 is used as a case study in identifying the specific causes of doubt, with Israel's example held up as a mirror for self-examination in the believer's own life.

context: we see the example in Numbers 13 of the children of Israel
point_summary: Numbers 13 is used as a case study in the causes of doubt, with Israel's example held up as a mirror for self-examination in the believer's own life.

context: if you read numbers 14 and you read about what happens there and their unbelief
point_summary: Unbelief in Numbers 14—Israel's refusal to trust God's ability to bring them into Canaan—is presented as the direct cause of 40 years of wilderness wandering, forfeiting the inheritance God had promised since Abraham.

context: Numbers 13 and 14, and we've been looking at this portion of scripture, these two chapters, over the last couple of weeks
point_summary: Numbers 13–14, examined over several weeks under the theme "a longing to see God work," frames the narrative as a witness to God's enduring power and active presence, as mighty today as he has ever been.

context: we've had the opportunity to look at these two chapters in Numbers 13 and 14
point_summary: Numbers 13–14 is treated as a historical case study in how the unbelief of God's people limits what an all-powerful God will do among them.

context: we've had the opportunity to look at these two chapters in Numbers 13 and 14
point_summary: Unbelief among God's people is identified as the limiting factor on what God does, with Numbers 13–14 serving as the historical case study for diagnosing what causes doubt.

context: The Bible begins in chapter 14 by talking about in all the congregation, in all the children of Israel, because of 10 people that did not believe God
point_summary: The doubt of ten spies in Numbers 14 spread to infect the entire nation of Israel, illustrating that unbelief is never private but carries harmful consequences for the wider community.

context: Beginning in verse number six of chapter 14, the Bible says that Joshua, it says, and Joshua the son of Nun and Caleb the son of Jephunneh, which were of them that searched the land, rent their clothes
point_summary: Joshua and Caleb's act of tearing their clothes signals a grief-stricken, counter-cultural response to the congregation's doubt-driven murmuring, held up as the model for confronting fear with faith.

context: It would be better for us to have stayed in Egypt and all of these things we've come here and our children, our wives, they're gonna be a prey and let us go back to Egypt in verse number four
point_summary: Doubt that overwhelms a congregation is pictured in Israel's cry to return to Egypt, the backdrop against which Joshua and Caleb's contrasting faith-response is examined.

context: the Bible says that they rent their clothes in verse seven, and they spake unto all the company of the children of Israel
point_summary: Against the fearful report of the ten spies, Joshua and Caleb's declaration that the land is "an exceeding good land" represents faith that refuses to be defined by obstacles rather than by God's promise.

context: it was surely it floweth with milk and honey, and this is the fruit of it
point_summary: Even the ten faithless spies confirmed the land's goodness, making their subsequent "nevertheless" of unbelief all the more inexcusable, since their obstacle-focused doubt was not rooted in ignorance of God's provision.

context: if the Lord delight in us, then he will bring us into this land and give it us, a land which floweth with milk and honey
point_summary: Where the ten spies said "we are able" or "we are not able," Joshua and Caleb shifted the question entirely to divine delight—God's pleasure secured through faith and obedience—as the true ground of conquest.

context: in chapter 13 in verse number two, it was God that said to Moses, get these men together, send them out to the land that I will give thee
point_summary: God's command in Numbers 13:2 to send spies into the land he promised to give establishes that the mission was always about divine promise, never human capability.

context: if the Lord delight in us, then he will bring us into this land and give it us a land which floweth with milk and honey
point_summary: Divine delight—expressed when God's people obey, trust, and live by faith—was Joshua and Caleb's basis for confidence that God, not their own ability, would deliver the promised land.

context: only rebel not ye against the Lord, neither fear ye the people of the land, for they are bred for us, their defenses departed from them, and the Lord is with us, fear them not
point_summary: Caleb and Joshua's call to "rebel not" and "fear them not" rests entirely on God's promise and presence, not on Israel's own ability to overcome the land's inhabitants.

context: for these people in numbers 13 and 14, what was God's will for them?
point_summary: When doubt and obstacles arise, the spies' failure in Numbers 13–14 illustrates what happens when God's people fix their eyes on hindrances rather than running to Scripture to confirm and obey God's known will.

context: and the Lord said unto Moses, how long, back in Numbers 14 and verse number 11, how long will this people provoke me and how long will it be ere they believe me for all the signs which I have showed among them?
point_summary: God's rebuke in Numbers 14:11—that Israel failed to believe despite witnessed signs—serves as a negative example driving the sermon's call to rehearse past divine blessings as a faith-building discipline against present doubt.

context: in verse number eight, bring us into this land
point_summary: Declaring faith aloud—as Caleb and Joshua did in affirming that God *will* bring them into the land—is presented as a necessary, active discipline for conquering doubt.

context: neither fear you the people of the land, for they are bred for us, their defenses departed from them, and the Lord is with us, fear them not
point_summary: Declaring faith verbally—as Joshua and Caleb did in Numbers 14:9, proclaiming the Lord's presence and commanding the people not to fear—is presented as a necessary, active step in conquering doubt.

context: if you look at Numbers chapter 14, when they doubted the possibility of going in and actually conquering the land
point_summary: God's grievance at Israel's unbelief in Numbers 14 — "How long will it be ere they believe me?" — is cited as evidence that God desires his people to trust him in the face of impossible circumstances.

context: How long will it be ere they believe me?
point_summary: God's grievance at Israel's unbelief in Numbers 14 — "How long will it be ere they believe me?" — is cited to show that God desires His people to trust Him, especially after He has already demonstrated His power on their behalf.

context: God is not a man that he should lie
point_summary: God's truthfulness, cited from Numbers 23:19, grounds the rejection of theistic evolution: if God had used evolution, he would have said so, and his silence on it confirms he did not lie in Genesis 1.

context: in Numbers 13 and 14, and we're going to study that over the next several Wednesdays, but in Numbers 13 and 14, we see what happens with the spies
point_summary: The ten spies' faithless report in Numbers 13–14 spread unbelief through the entire congregation, resulting in divine judgment—death in the wilderness—because God's promise of the land required faith, not assessment of obstacles.

context: in Numbers 13 and 14, and we're going to study that over the next several Wednesdays, but in Numbers 13 and 14, we see what happens with the spies
point_summary: Israel's unbelief in Numbers 13–14, where ten spies spread a faithless report that turned the whole congregation against God's promise, resulted in divine judgment and death in the wilderness—illustrating that God takes seriously whether his people believe him.

context: there in Numbers 14, that they were going to die
point_summary: Israel's wilderness generation died in judgment not because the obstacles were too great, but because they refused to believe God's promise to give them the land.

context: go over to Numbers chapter 12 if you will. In Numbers chapter 12 there's an event that takes place
point_summary: Moses's intercessory cry for Miriam's healing in Numbers 12:13 is presented as the direct fruit of his prior knowledge of God as Jehovah Rapha—one cannot pray for healing without first knowing God as the God who heals.

context: in verse number 10 where the Bible says, you know, the cloud departed from off the tabernacle and behold Miriam, notice, became leprous, white as snow and Aaron looked upon Miriam and behold she was leprous
point_summary: Moses's intercessory cry for Miriam's healing is presented as the direct, practical fruit of already knowing God as Jehovah Rapha—revelation of who God is grounds and motivates the prayer for healing.

context: as we've looked at numbers 13 and 14
point_summary: Doubt and unbelief limit God's working, and Numbers 13–14 serves as the biblical case study for how that limitation operates in the lives of God's people.

context: as we've looked at numbers 13 and 14
point_summary: Doubt and unbelief, as illustrated in the Israel narrative of Numbers 13–14, actively limit what God will do, just as Psalm 78 declares that Israel "limited the Holy One of Israel."

context: In Numbers chapter 13, beginning in verse number one, the Bible says, and the Lord spake unto Moses saying
point_summary: Doubt is not a harmless condition but an active sin that must be rooted out, because its consequences—illustrated by the twelve spies' mission—inevitably spread beyond the individual to impact others.

context: It says in verse 16, these are the names of the men which Moses sent to spy out the land
point_summary: Doubt's communal consequences are introduced through the twelve spies' mission, with Numbers 13:16 serving as the roster verse establishing the men whose unbelief would devastate an entire nation.

context: verse number two says, of every tribe of their fathers, shall you send a man
point_summary: God's command to send one leader from each tribe established the very structure that allowed ten doubting spies to spread their unbelief and drag an entire nation into consequence.

context: The Bible tells us in verse number 27, and they told him, that's the spies as they return
point_summary: Doubt's contagion is illustrated by how the ten spies' unbelief, pivoting on the word "nevertheless," shifted the entire report from God's promise to the obstacles, ultimately spreading that doubt to the whole congregation.

context: they say this in verse 28, nevertheless, and it changes direction
point_summary: Doubt shifts focus from God's promise to present obstacles, as the ten spies' "nevertheless" pivoted from the land's goodness to walled cities and giants, ultimately producing a faithless conclusion that spread to others.

context: we be not able to go up against the people for they are stronger than we
point_summary: Doubt voiced by a minority — here ten out of twelve spies — can spread to an entire congregation, as the ten's conclusion that the people were too strong to overcome triggered corporate weeping, murmuring, and despair throughout Israel.

context: Caleb here in chapter number 13 in verse 30, he tries to steal the people
point_summary: Caleb's attempt to still the people (v. 30) stands in contrast to the ten spies whose doubt spread contagiously until the entire congregation wept, murmured, and wished for death rather than the promised land.

context: and all the congregation lifted up their voice and cried and the people wept that night
point_summary: Doubt, even from a small minority, spreads rapidly and devastatingly through a community, as ten men's unbelief moved an entire congregation to weeping, murmuring, and despair.

context: verse two, and all the children of Israel murmured against Moses and against Aaron and the whole congregation said unto them, would God that we had died in the land of Egypt or would God we had died in this wilderness
point_summary: The murmuring of ten doubting spies spread until the whole congregation—potentially millions—turned against God's promise, illustrating that personal doubt carries devastating corporate consequences.

context: verse number 10, it says, but all the congregation bade stone them with stones and the glory of the Lord appeared in the tabernacle of the congregation before all the children of Israel
point_summary: Doubt's contagion is traced from ten faithless spies to a congregation of millions calling for stoning, demonstrating that unbelief spreads and ensnares entire communities across generations.

context: there's 10 men in chapter number 13
point_summary: The unbelief of ten spies spread from a small number to an entire congregation of potentially millions, illustrating that personal doubt carries corporate consequences across generations.

context: when we come to chapter number 14, it says in all the congregation, it says that the whole congregation, all the children of Israel, it says all the congregation bade stone them with stones
point_summary: The doubt of ten spies spread to an entire congregation of potentially millions, illustrating that personal unbelief does not remain private but cascades outward to infect and devastate those who follow.

context: you can look in the beginning of Numbers chapter one, you can begin to see the numbers
point_summary: The census figures in Numbers 1, totaling likely several million Israelites, are cited to show the staggering scale of influence that the ten doubting spies had on an entire nation.

context: we see it here in Numbers 13 and 14, very, very obvious to us
point_summary: The doubt of ten spies spread to an entire congregation of millions, demonstrating that unbelief is never private but inevitably contaminates those around us.

context: verse number four of Numbers 14, notice what it says, this congregation is speaking against the leadership and it says in verse number four, and they said one to another, let us make a captain and let us return into Egypt
point_summary: Unbelief does not merely stall the individual — it actively reverses direction, as Israel's proposal to appoint a new captain and return to Egypt illustrates the failure to press forward that doubt inevitably produces.

context: let us make a captain and let us return into Egypt
point_summary: Doubt and unbelief so distort reasoning that Israel proposed returning to Egypt without accounting for the impossibility of crossing the Red Sea again on their own.

context: I want you to hold your place here in numbers
point_summary: Doubt and unbelief cause God's people to retreat toward former bondage rather than advance in faith, forfeiting the opportunity to witness what an omnipotent God can do.

context: and have tempted me now these 10 times and have not hearkened to my voice
point_summary: Repeated failure to hearken to God's voice—rooted in unbelief—results in being given over to one's own counsel and forfeiting the sight of what God can do.

context: It says in verse number 11, the Lord said unto Moses, how long will this people provoke me? And how long will it be ere they believe me? For all the signs which I have showed among them.
point_summary: God's repeated faithfulness to Israel makes their unbelief all the more grievous, as His question — "how long will they not believe me?" — exposes the contradiction of trusting Him for eternity while doubting His provision today.

context: Surely they shall not see the land which I swear unto their fathers, neither shall any of them that provoked me see it
point_summary: Doubt and unbelief cost Israel the very blessings God had sworn to give them — they would not see the promised land, forfeiting what Caleb, who followed God fully, inherited.

context: but my servant Caleb, because he had another spirit with him, and it followed me fully, him will I bring into the land whereunto he went, and his seed shall possess it
point_summary: Caleb's wholehearted following of God, contrasted with Israel's unbelief, is presented as the reason he alone received the promised blessing the doubting congregation forfeited.

context: verse 25, the Amalekites and the Canaanites dwelt in the valley. Tomorrow turn you and get you into the wilderness by the way of the Red Sea.
point_summary: Doubt and murmuring carry concrete, generational consequences: Israel's unbelief cost the entire numbered generation their inheritance, while the very children they feared for became the ones who received it.

context: And the Lord spake unto Moses and unto Aaron, saying, How long shall I bear with this evil congregation which murmur against me? I have heard the murmurings of the children of Israel, which they murmured against me, saying to them, As truly as I live, saith the Lord, as ye have spoken in mine ears, so will I do to you. Your carcasses shall fall in the wilderness, and all that were numbered of you, according to your whole number, from twenty years old and upward, which have murmured against me. Doubtless ye shall not come into the land concerning which I swear to make you dwell therein, save Caleb the son of Jephunneh, and Joshua the son of Nun.
point_summary: Doubt and murmuring carry concrete, generational consequences: God declared that the very generation who complained would die in the wilderness and forfeit the promised land, while the children they feared for would inherit it instead.

context: But your little ones, which ye said should be a prey
point_summary: God's word proved exactly true: the very children the doubting Israelites feared would be destroyed were the ones He brought into the land they had despised.

context: And wherefore hath the Lord brought us unto this land to fall by the sword, that our wives and our children should be a prey? Were it not better for us to return unto Egypt?
point_summary: Doubting Israel's fearful claim that their children would be prey was answered by God's ironic decree that those very children — not the doubters — would inherit the land.

context: this generation of people that we're reading of in Numbers 13 and 14
point_summary: The doubt and unbelief of the ten spies, which swayed the entire nation, cost that generation the promised land entirely—wilderness wandering and death were never God's intended plan for them.

context: this generation of people that we're reading of in Numbers 13 and 14
point_summary: The generation that doubted God's promise in Numbers 14 died in the wilderness and forfeited the very blessings—Jordan's crossing, Jericho's conquest—that the next generation experienced, because doubt, not divine intention, caused the forty years of wandering.

context: the generation that we're speaking of in numbers 13 and 14, the generation that was marked by doubt and unbelief
point_summary: The generation marked by doubt and unbelief in Numbers 13–14 forfeited the very blessings—conquest, land, and visible displays of God's power—that the faithful generation of Joshua later received.

context: we be not able
point_summary: Doubt displaced God's promise from view: the spies' confession "we be not able" reveals that they had shifted their focus from what God declared He would give to the size of the obstacles before them.

context: Joshua and Caleb and those spies brought back when they searched out the land in Canaan and they brought so many grapes that it had to be between two men carried on a stave
point_summary: The abundance of grapes found by the spies in Canaan (Numbers 13:23) is cited as evidence that Israel had native grapes and therefore no need to import fermented wine.
